source: Infant preference for attractive animal faces (see corpus: 02-attractive-faces/attractive-faces.source-card.md)

questions.prompt: Researchers showed 3–4-month-old infants pairs of domestic cat faces — one rated attractive by adults, one unattractive. Which face did the infants look at longer?
options: No reliable difference — babies are too young to care
questions.explanation: Infants looked longer at the attractive cat faces: 61.25% mean preference, with 16 of 20 infants above chance. The preference for attractive faces extends beyond human faces.

questions.prompt: Then the researchers turned the same cat faces upside down. What happened to the preference?
options: It stayed — an attractive image is attractive either way
questions.explanation: It vanished — 51.87%, chance level. So the preference isn't about raw image properties like brightness or contrast. It depends on the upright structural configuration of a face.

questions.prompt: Wild tiger faces — animals no infant has met. Shown upright, did 3–4-month-olds prefer the attractive tigers too?
options: No — unfamiliar wild faces didn't trigger it
questions.explanation: Yes: 61.45% preference upright (and only 52.87% inverted — gone again). Even for wild animals, infant perception is drawn to attractive, well-formed upright faces.

takeaway: Attractive faces sit close to the prototype — the mathematical average of many faces. Before culture, before language, human perception already anchors on the statistical center of a category.
